summary:
The Baden culture, flourishing in central and southeastern Europe — principally in modern Hungary, Slovakia, Austria, and neighboring regions — between approximately 3600 and 2800 BCE, was one of the most widespread late Neolithic and Copper Age cultural complexes of Europe. Named after the site of Baden near Vienna, this culture is characterized by distinctive pottery with channeled or fluted decoration, and by evidence for the earliest use of wheeled vehicles and secondary animal products (milk, wool, traction) in central Europe.

The Baden culture represents a significant transformation in the relationship between humans and domestic animals. Evidence from this period includes the first clear attestations of the use of cattle for traction (pulling plows and wagons) rather than purely for meat, and the development of woolen textiles. This Secondary Products Revolution — as archaeologist Andrew Sherratt termed it — fundamentally changed agricultural economics, allowing farmers to exploit domestic animals as renewable resources (wool, milk, traction) rather than one-time meat sources, substantially increasing the productivity and sustainability of farming communities.

Baden material culture shows connections to the contemporaneous [[event:1652|Yamnaya culture]] of the Pontic steppe, including shared elements of burial rite and possibly the adoption of wheeled vehicle technology. Some scholars have proposed that the Baden culture represents a zone of early contact between the incoming steppe groups and established central European farming populations. Baden sites are widely distributed and show considerable regional variation, but the cultural package is coherent enough to suggest meaningful social and economic connections across a large area.

The Baden culture ended around 2800 BCE, transitioning into the archaeological cultures that immediately preceded the [[event:1651|Corded Ware culture]] horizon. Its legacy includes the adoption of secondary animal products and wheeled vehicles across central Europe.
